speaker: interviewee
text: You will probably edit all this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nope. Nope.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in perspective?

speaker: interviewer
text: Nope. Nope. Nope.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just like I said?

speaker: interviewer
text: Exactly how you say it, that's how it's going to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: No editing, no cutting, no nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because sometimes I went back to--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. That's all right. It's OK. If you remember something, you want to say it, absolutely. Because we want it to be totally natural, just from your point of view.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's what I'm saying. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now tell me about the Likud. I want to know what you did. Well, I was a soldier in the Likud. And it didn't take long after the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] United Nations gave Palestine the name of Israel, which Ben-Gurion and the Knesset, they decided that this is going to be called Israel. And all the organizations-- like the Haganah and the Likud and the Palmach and the Lohamei Herut Israel-- all of those organizations become the first military units in the Israel army. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

words.text: I want to know what you did.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had-- you want to know about Likud? Only about Likud? Everything you want to tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Likud. The Likud is-- when we fought against the British, we bombed the oil refineries in Haifa. We bombed the CID Building in Jaffa-- Jaffa, Tel Aviv. Our people did it. And naturally, they-- I was all over there, when I had to go. And we did everything possible to get them out of Palestine. That was the Likud's work. The Likud's work for me was short because I came to Israel in 1945. And in 1947, it was already a war between the organizations like Palmach, like Likud, with the British. And then we-- so we had a lot of the actions. They took us to the orange groves. In the orange groves they used to train new soldiers, and new soldiers-- all not legally. And the Arabs started burning our houses. In fact, she was pregnant and I took her-- I was that time at home. I took her over the bridge to Tel Aviv. She could almost not make it. And the worst part was the British. They used to go into kibbutzim and arrest people. They used to arrest everybody, whoever they want. They knew. But we were underground, and they couldn't do nothing about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was a lot of action going on in the underground. We even used to go into the Arab cities and did what we had to do and get out. They were very belligerent to us, like they are today in the real country where they are. And so we fought them. We fought them and we did what we had to do. And they run. They took their donkeys and a couple of chairs and they run. They called us devils because we were not like the Israeli Jews. I mean, we came from concentration camp. We were rough. We were tough, rough. We didn't know what it means to be afraid. This is our country. So we did a lot of things which had to be done. It can't be mentioned all the time. I think a lot of people will tell you about it. We-- I was in Tel Aviv, and it was between Tel Aviv and Hatikvah, was a cement factory. And they used to do blocks, you know, blocks what you build buildings. The Arabs went in there one night and they killed all of them. Not enough they kill them, but they cut pieces with a knife-- pieces-- and they put them together in one [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] bundle. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so that came to our ears. Those Arabs were coming from the village of Deir Yassin. I want to emphasize Deir Yassin was a very blood bath. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was the day when Deir Yassin was completely, completely-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everybody was killed there, by us, by us, our people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We believed that time one eye for an eye because that was too much. Take 14 people and to cut them in pieces. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I get a drink, maybe? So this was in retaliation for killing?

speaker: interviewee
text: For killing 14 people. They kill them. Not only they kill them, but they cut pieces with a knife. Every piece from that body was cut in little pieces. And they put them together in one bundle like it would be a haystack, or whatever. Well, that took a lot of guts to do it. It took for us a lot of guts to do what we did. From that time on, they run. They couldn't believe that Jews could do it. They run. They run. They took their cows, their pigs, whatever they had there. They run. It was-- it took no time. No Arab was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's how we-- that's how we got our land back. That was before we got-- United Nations gave us our-- in between. And what else you want to know from me? Then what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the Deir Yassin? The British started to back off. The Arabs run. And I was in the army at that time. That was already Israeli Army. The British gave the Arabs the only strategic points. You know, the police-- all police departments in Israel are in a high ground. That is strategic in the area. So they can shoot wherever they want to, if they need to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that was-- we went to Latrun. You know? You heard about it? [LAUGHS] And I was probably three times in Latrun.

speaker: interviewee
text: One time we couldn't get it. We couldn't take it. It was very hard to take. They were on the underground, the high ground. They had Jordan's army was there helping them. They had big, big guns. But one time we went in and we got it. But then, before we got it, we didn't go in right away on this spot, because we were afraid that somebody is giving us an ambush or something like that. So-- but we went first and we took out our wounded ones. I took out one. He was [? Kabul ?] Israel. His name was Simantov. He was without two legs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I carried him, his legs was hanging on piece of skins. And I got him out. I was already a father at that time. Got him out. And we went on to the other cities, on to Lod. On to Lod, the airport. We still have pictures when we got the first plane from Europe. The first plane. So we have a picture of that. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And we had little tanks which came from Czechoslovakia. That was given by Rothschild. Rothschild had a lot of ground in Czechoslovakia. So he exchanged it, exchanged it with leaders. Exchanged it between ground and they gave us tanks, little tanks. Maybe they-- we had in our tank division, we had-- this was the only one in Israel-- we had maybe four tanks. And one tank couldn't shoot, only with the gun, with a machine gun. The others could shoot, but we went. We went ahead and we did everything possible yet to get them out. And we got them out. We got the Arabs out where they are. They came back later. Some Arabs came back later to live there because they had still their houses. But most of them, you know the rest, where they are. They're coming back now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else you want to know? I have a lot of stories to tell you. I want to hear all the stories. Tell me any story you want. We want to hear everything. Whatever you want to tell.

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess this was my life between 1940-- 1940 to 1945. We were liberated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What I did in Israel, that was already after those things happened. But we had a miserable, miserable life. I was my own-- on my own. I was-- I didn't know that I have somebody in the world. But I remembered I had my father in Israel, in Palestine, through Palestine. But, otherwise, that's about what I can tell you. We lived in Israel for 16 years. I was very happy. We were happy. The kids were happy. We weren't millionaires, but we existed. We had a little business. We existed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we decided to join her sister. Her sister was here in United States. So they send us papers and we came to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what that was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Coming to United States? Was like coming to Heaven. We came from Israel. Well, it wasn't so easy to get out of Israel, if you want to know exactly how it was. They told us that you can go, you can join your sister-in-law, your sister, but you cannot take the kids away from here. So we had to fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if I didn't get a heart attack on the airport-- on the port, not the airport. We went with a ship, a vessel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My son was on your passport, right? On my wife passport. And somehow, someplace, it was a mistake then that he's not on this paper and another paper. So they wanted him to stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all packed. We were all on ship. And they insisted us to leave him here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother-in-law was a taxi driver. He had a taxi in Israel. So he said to me, you know what? Why don't we go to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Secretary of Transport, like here. Over there it's different. It's spelled in Hebrew different. He said, I'll get it. I know where the guy lives. Let's go to this man. Let's find out what we can do. They're not going to let you go like this. We sold our house. We sold our everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to that man. That man was a very religious man. And when you come into a secretary of whatever he is, you have to have patience. And here, I don't have any patience. I don't have any time.

speaker: interviewee
text: The captain of the ship told us-- he guessed that something is wrong with one of the kids, and they don't want to let him go. He said to me, Mr. Shapow, you go ahead. We'll wait for you. They waited for us couple hours. And we went to that man. He used to eat-- he was eating. He was eating. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after he finished eating-- you know, a religious man, if he eats, you don't bother him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he called me up, called me and said to me, what can I do for you? I told him the story. He said, do you have a vehicle? I said yes. He said, I'll go with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The guy was 6 times 5, I mean he's 6 foot 5, tall guy. And he went with me. As soon as they saw him in the port, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they said, Mar Shapow, Mr. Shapow, everything is straightened out for [YIDDISH]. Everything is OK. Everything's fine. You're going. That was heaven, getting out of this. And I went to United States. We went to Atlanta, met our sister-in-law. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't like the place, Atlanta. And at that time, still used to-- the black people used to work on the street. 1960, imagine. And I didn't like it. It bring me-- brought me bad memories. I was working on the streets in Riga. I mean, not in sidewalk, in street. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went away from there. Went to Chicago. We had a cousin there. And I got work to do there. And I bought a business later on, and a bigger business. And then she got sick. And the doctors told her you've got to get out of here because it's too cold for you. And we went out. We break-- I went to the landlord. I had probably $40,000, $50,000 that time at the shelves. Went to the landlord and I put in new cases. And it's a big, big market I had. I said to the landlord, look, I got to break the lease anyhow, but I want to break it in nice way. Nicest way is going to be that I'm going to leave you all the cases here-- the electrical, the refrigerators, you know, like in the market. I am going to leave it to you. You can then list it as a market. But I'm going to take all my stuff away from you, so I can breathe afterwards. And that's what I did. I went away from him. We went to-- they told us to go to a warm climate-- back to Israel, or to Arizona, or to Los Angeles. We had cousins here, cousins which I never met in my life. My father had six, seven brothers, two sisters. They were all here. One brother was in Europe. He got killed. But when we came here, they were all dead, except the kids were alive. We had some pictures we got somehow together. And that's how we started a life in Los Angeles. And we had a hectic life here, too. Thank God we're here. Kids are married, have grandchildren. We have each other, me and my wife. And that's the end of my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's cut right here.

speaker: interviewee
text: With my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. This we're going to show.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Should I say my name?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Hela. Hela Shapow. And I will introduce her to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've been married how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are married 48 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Mister Shapow, almost 50 years after the war, do you have a message that you'd like to share with us for your children and for your grandchildren and for the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: A message of love?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. A message--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of love, of life. Whatever you'd like to say about your life and what you went through. Any message.

speaker: interviewee
text: A message? My message will be for the world to stop fighting, would any ethnic groups or any ethnic stop fighting, because life is short. Peace is the most-- the most precious word in life is peace. And if peace is in the family, and peace is in the country, and peace would be all over the world, it would be a different life for everybody. Better life. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken when I was in my mother's womb, as she was pregnant with me. And this is-- my mother is Haya Hodel. My father is Chaim Mordechai. One is born-- my mother's born 1904. My father is born in 1901. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Those are my parents.
